message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Rapid antigen tests have been used to prevent the spread of the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19); however, there have been concerns about their decreased sensitivity to the Omicron variant.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aims</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this study, we compared the sensitivity and specificity of the rapid antigen and the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) tests among the players and staff members of the Japan Professional Football League and clubs. Furthermore, we evaluated the relationship between the sensitivity and the duration from the onset of the symptoms to testing, the manufacturer of the rapid antigen test kits, and the PCR test analyte.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design and methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This was a retrospective observational study. We used 656 results from both the rapid antigen and PCR tests for COVID-19 using the analytes collected on the same day from January 12 to March 2, 2022, during the Omicron variant outbreak in Japan.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The sensitivity of the rapid antigen test compared with the PCR test was 0.63 (95% confidence interval: 0.54–0.72) and the specificity was 0.998 (95% confidence interval: 0.995–1.000). There were no significant associations between the sensitivity and the duration from the onset of the symptoms to testing (including asymptomatic cases in the category), vaccination status, manufacturer of the rapid antigen test kit or PCR analyte (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    > 0.05) with small effect sizes (Cramer’s V or φ: ≤ 0.22).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Even during the Omicron outbreak, the sensitivity of the rapid antigen tests did not depend on the duration from the onset of the symptoms to testing.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Sensitivity of rapid antigen tests for COVID-19 during the Omicron variant outbreak

reference.article-title: Laboratory diagnosis of emerging human coronavirus infections – the state of the art
